title: Lebkuchen
subtitle: German honey spice biscuits, glazed with dark chocolate
excerpt: Soft, aromatic German gingerbread biscuits made with honey, brown sugar, and a full mixture of warming spices. The dough is made a day or two ahead and rested, which deepens the spice flavour. Baked at a low temperature to keep them chewy, then glazed with melted dark chocolate or a simple sugar icing. A classic Christmas biscuit that keeps well in a tin for two weeks.
sourceNotes: Lebkuchen (honey cake biscuits) have been made in German-speaking regions since the medieval period. Nuremberg is particularly associated with the tradition; the Nürnberger Lebkuchen received protected designation of origin status in the EU, though the general recipe is documented in German cookbooks from the early nineteenth century. Henriette Davidis, Praktisches Kochbuch (1844, Project Gutenberg), gives a Lebkuchen recipe with honey, spices, and a resting period consistent with the modern approach. The combination of cinnamon, ginger, cloves, pepper, and honey is consistent across German baking literature from that period.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked, unglazed Lebkuchen for up to 2 months. Glaze after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the dough at least a day ahead and refrigerate. Two to three days is better for flavour development. Baked Lebkuchen keep well in an airtight tin for 2 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Bake for 12 to 16 minutes until just set; the biscuits should feel firm at the edges but still give slightly in the centre. They firm up completely as they cool.
baking.decoratingTechnique: dark chocolate glaze or sugar icing

recipeTools.notes: two baking trays, lined with baking paper

glossaryTerms.definition: Leaving Lebkuchen dough in the fridge for 24 to 72 hours before rolling and baking. During this time the honey and spices penetrate the flour and the flavour develops significantly. Freshly made dough produces bland, flat-tasting biscuits; rested dough produces deeply spiced, aromatic ones. The rest also firms up the dough (honey is hygroscopic and makes the dough sticky initially), making it easier to roll and cut. The longer the rest, within reason, the better the flavour.

content.text: The key to Lebkuchen flavour is
for at least 24 hours and ideally 2 to 3 days before rolling. The dough is made quickly; the rest does the work. Do not overbake: Lebkuchen should remain slightly chewy at the centre when cold.

The day before (or up to 3 days ahead): gently warm the honey, sugar, and butter in a small saucepan until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves. Leave to cool to room temperature. Stir in the eggs, then mix in the flour, bicarbonate of soda, and all the spices to form a smooth, slightly sticky dough. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for at least 24 hours.

When ready to bake: heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Line two trays with baking paper. On a lightly floured surface, roll the cold dough to about 4mm thick and cut into rounds or shapes. Place on the trays, press a blanched almond into each. Bake for 12 to 16 minutes until firm at the edges and just set in the centre. Cool on a rack; they will firm up further as they cool.

Glaze: melt the dark chocolate in a bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water. Dip one side of each cold biscuit in the chocolate or brush it on with a pastry brush. Set on baking paper to harden. Store in an airtight tin, interleaved with baking paper, for up to 2 weeks.
